From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

 

 
(Redirected from Deep Isolation)

 
 
 

Long term storage for radioactive and hazardous waste

Technicians emplacing transuranic waste at the Waste Isolation Pilot Plant, near Carlsbad, New Mexico
A deep geological repository is a way of storing hazardous or radioactive waste within a stable geologic environment, typically 200–1,000 m underground.[1] It entails a combination of waste form, waste package, engineered seals and geology that is suited to provide a high level of long-term isolation and containment without future maintenance. This is intended to prevent radioactive dangers.[2] A number of mercury, cyanide and arsenic waste repositories are operating worldwide including Canada (Giant Mine) and Germany (potash mines in Herfa-Neurode and Zielitz).[3] Radioactive waste storage sites are under construction with the Onkalo in Finland being the most advanced.[4]

Principles and background
[edit]

Highly toxic waste that cannot be further recycled must be stored in isolation, to avoid contamination of air, ground and underground water. Deep geological repository is a type of long-term storage that isolates waste in geological structures that are expected to be stable for millions of years, with a number of natural and engineered barriers. Natural barriers include water-impermeable (e.g. clay) and gas-impermeable (e.g. salt) layers of rock above and surrounding the underground storage.[3] Engineered barriers include bentonite clay and cement.[1][5]

In 2011, the International Panel on Fissile Materials said:

It is widely accepted that spent nuclear fuel and high-level reprocessing and plutonium wastes require well-designed storage for periods ranging from tens of thousands to a million years, to minimize releases of the contained radioactivity into the environment. Safeguards are also required to ensure that neither plutonium nor highly enriched uranium is diverted to weapon use. There is general agreement that placing spent nuclear fuel in repositories hundreds of meters below the surface would be safer than indefinite storage of spent fuel on the surface [of the earth].[6]

Common elements of repositories include the radioactive waste, the containers enclosing the waste, other engineered barriers or seals around the containers, the tunnels housing the containers, and the geologic makeup of the surrounding area.[7]

A storage space hundreds of metres below the ground needs to withstand the effects of one or more future glaciations, with thick ice sheets resting on top of the rock.[8][9] The presence of ice sheets affects the hydrostatic pressure at repository depth, groundwater flow and chemistry, and the potential for earthquakes. This is being taken into consideration by organizations preparing for long-term waste repositories in Sweden, Finland, Canada and some other countries that have to assess the effects of future glaciations.[9]

Despite a long-standing agreement among many experts that geological disposal can be safe, technologically feasible and environmentally sound, a large part of the general public in many countries remains skeptical as a result of anti-nuclear campaigns.[10] One of the challenges facing the supporters of these efforts is to demonstrate confidently that a repository will contain wastes for so long that any releases that might take place in the future will pose no significant health or environmental risk.

Nuclear reprocessing does not eliminate the need for a repository, but reduces the volume, the long-term radiation hazard, and long-term heat dissipation capacity needed. Reprocessing does not eliminate the political and community challenges to repository siting.[6]
See also: High-level radioactive waste management

Natural radioactive repositories
[edit]

Natural uranium ore deposits serve as proof of concept for stability of radioactive elements in geological formations—Cigar Lake Mine for example is a natural deposit of highly concentrated uranium ore located under sandstone and a quartz layer at a depth of 450 m, that is 1 billion years old with no radioactive leaks to the surface.[11]

A Swedish KBS-3 capsule for nuclear waste
The ability of natural geologic barriers to isolate radioactive waste is demonstrated by the natural nuclear fission reactors at Oklo, Gabon. During their long reaction period about 5.4 tonnes of fission products as well as 1.5 tonnes of plutonium together with other transuranic elements were generated in the uranium ore body. This plutonium and the other transuranics remained immobile until the present day, a span of almost 2 billion years.[12] This is remarkable as ground water had ready access to the deposits and they were not in a chemically inert form, such as glass.[citation needed]

Research
[edit]

Deep geologic disposal has been studied for several decades, including laboratory tests, exploratory boreholes, and the construction and operation of underground research laboratories where large-scale in-situ tests are being conducted.[13] Major underground test facilities are listed below.

Status of repository at certain sites
[edit]

A schematic of a geologic repository under construction at Olkiluoto Nuclear Power Plant site, Finland
A demonstration tunnel in Olkiluoto.
In February 2014, radioactive materials leaked from a damaged storage drum at the U. S. Waste Isolation Pilot Plant. Analysis of several accidents, by the U.S. Department Of Energy, have shown lack of a "safety culture".[31]
The process of selecting appropriate deep final repositories is under way in several countries, with the first expected to be commissioned some time after 2010.[32][needs update]

Australia
[edit]

There was a proposal in the early 2000s for an international high level waste repository in Australia[33] and Russia.[34] Since the proposal for a global repository in Australia, which has never produced nuclear power, and has one research reactor, was raised, domestic political objections have been loud and sustained, making such a facility in Australia unlikely.

Canada
[edit]

Main article: Giant Mine

Giant Mine has been used as a deep repository for storage of highly toxic arsenic waste in the form of powder. As of 2020 there is ongoing research to reprocess the waste into a frozen block form which is more chemically stable and prevents water contamination.[35]

On Nov 28, 2024, the NWMO selected the Wabigoon Lake Ojibway Nation-Ignace area as the site for Canada's deep geological repository for used nuclear fuel.[36][37]

Finland
[edit]

The Onkalo site in Finland based on the KBS-3 technology, is the furthest along the road to becoming operational among repositories worldwide. Posiva started construction of the site in 2004. The Finnish government issued the company a licence for constructing the final disposal facility in November 2015. Posiva has applied for an operating licence to begin disposals, but As of March 2026[update], the response from the regulator, STUK, is delayed until the end of June 2026.[38]

Germany
[edit]

A number of repositories including potash mines in Herfa-Neurode and Zielitz have been used for years for the storage of highly toxic mercury, cyanide and arsenic waste.[3] There is little debate in Germany regarding toxic waste, in spite of the fact that unlike nuclear waste, it does not lose toxicity with time.

There is a debate about the search for a final repository for radioactive waste, accompanied by protests, especially in the Gorleben village in the Wendland area, which was seen as ideal for the final repository until 1990 because of its location in a remote, economically depressed corner of West Germany, next to the closed border to the former East Germany. After reunification, the village is now close to the center of Germany, and is now used for temporary storage of nuclear waste.

The pit Asse II is a former salt mine in the mountain range of Asse in Lower Saxony/Germany, that was allegedly used as a research mine since 1965. Between 1967 and 1978, radioactive waste was placed in storage. Research indicated that brine contaminated with radioactive caesium-137, plutonium and strontium was leaking from the mine since 1988 but was not reported until June 2008.[39] The repository for radioactive waste Morsleben is a deep geological repository for radioactive waste in the rock salt mine Bartensleben in Morsleben, in Saxony-Anhalt/Germany, that was used from 1972 to 1998. Since 2003, 480,000 m3 (630,000 cu yd) of salt-concrete has been pumped into the pit to temporarily stabilize the upper levels.

Sweden
[edit]

Approval was granted in January 2022 for the construction of a direct disposal facility using KBS-3 technology, on the site of the Forsmark nuclear power plant.[40]

About Us

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Create a greener, cleaner, safer planet from space
 

 

 

 

 
Core industries have billions of distributed assets and spend hundreds of billions of dollars on operations, maintenance, and sustainability. In fact, in the U.S. alone, power utilities themselves spend $200 billion annually on operations and maintenance.
But core industries lose billions of dollars every year due to complex factors affecting these distributed assets because they are:
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 

 
Hyperconnected and geographically dispersed 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Affected by weather and surroundings 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 
Aging, along with experienced people in the workforce 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Subject to complex regulatory compliance 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Read More

 
Existing software systems have failed to transform these industries’ operations, maintenance, and sustainability. Legacy systems are archaic, monolithic, and expensive, and don’t support data-driven decisions.

 
But satellites can monitor these assets at scale and transform operations, maintenance, and sustainability. Satellite launches are nearing 1,000 annually. And satellite technology is future-proof, with ever-improving imagery capabilities. 

 
AiDASH is doing this today with more than 150 customers of every size around the world. AiDASH helps these core industries become more resilient, efficient, and sustainable through the power of satellites and AI.

Our Leadership Team
Chef’s team of senior leaders has scaled an array of software and hardware products.

Rajat Bhageria
CEO

Rajat Bhageria
CEO
Rajat is the Founder and CEO of Chef Robotics. Prior to Chef, Rajat was Founder and Managing Partner at Prototype Capital, a pre-seed venture capital fund investing in founders who apply new technology to old industries. Before that, Rajat was the Founder and CEO of ThirdEye, where he led a team to build a product that empowers the visually impaired by recognizing what’s in front of them; ThirdEye was ultimately sold. Rajat has written a book about education reform, published a scientific paper in electrical engineering, and written for Forbes about entrepreneurship. He has a Master’s degree in Robotics and Machine Learning from the University of Pennsylvania and a Bachelor’s in Economics from The Wharton School at the University of Pennsylvania.

Ray Martino
COO

Ray Martino
COO
Prior to Chef, Ray served as the COO and Co-Founder of Specific Diagnostics from 2013 until its acquisition by bioMerieux Inc. in 2022 for $425M. During his tenure, he led development, manufacturing, and operations, with responsibilities extending to marketing, customer engagements, fundraising, finance, and business development at different stages of the company's growth. 
Before Specific, Ray was at Symbol Technologies, acquired by Motorola Solutions, and finally acquired by Zebra Technologies (NASDAQ: ZBRA); here, he served in increasing positions from engineer to VP of Engineering to General Manager to SVP Marketing and finally CTO over 14 years. During his time at Symbol, the company grew from $20M to over $2B in annual revenue. As a General Manager, he led teams that launched products – sold to manufacturing companies and retail companies – supporting annual revenues of over $1.5B. Under his leadership, Symbol pioneered mobile computers, barcode scanners, and wireless networks (WiFi), becoming the first-to-market with WiFi products.

Somudro Gupta
Head of AI

Somudro Gupta
Head of AI
Prior to Chef, Somudro was Head of Autonomous Vehicle Engineering at Embark Trucks, a publicly traded company that was acquired by Applied Intuition in 2022. Over his five year tenure, Somudro joined as the first software engineer where he built much of Embark’s autonomy stack; he was then promoted to lead the Motion Planning and Controls team; he then was further promoted to lead the entire autonomy division at Embark, representing 45 software engineers. Prior to Embark, Somudro was at Volkswagen Electronics Research Laboratory; he was the US Motion Planning Lead for Jack, a self-driving Audi A7 that drove 550 miles from the Bay Area to Las Vegas for CES 2015 (the longest demonstrated autonomous highway drive at the time).

John Unkovic
Head of Hardware

John Unkovic
Head of Hardware
John is a technical leader, with a hardware engineering background, who has designed and built a variety of physical products. Particularly in the computer vision and automation space. John has experience setting up global supply chains (particularly in Asia), working with contract manufacturers and building internal manufacturing infrastructure and teams. 
His most recent experience includes building the hardware team and product at Third Wave Automation, an autonomous forklift company. He also, at Stockwell, grew the hardware team from zero to 30 team members responsible for hardware design, engineering, manufacturing, and field maintenance. They deployed over 1,500 units to four major metro areas in the US before an acquisition. 

Kartheek Chandu
Head of Software

Kartheek Chandu
Head of Software
Kartheek is Chef's Head of Software. 
Before joining Chef, Kartheek led sensor signal processing at Cruise, where he directed the development of advanced imaging systems for autonomous vehicles and optimized crucial data pipelines for machine learning models. 
At Light Labs, he built and enhanced top-tier engineering capabilities, successfully launching the innovative multi-aperture camera system (L16) and pioneering the 5-camera smartphone system (Nokia 9).
Earlier in his career, Kartheek contributed to technical excellence across multiple flagship camera generations at GoPro while developing the company's first proprietary System-on-Chip for the consumer market. 
He began his professional journey at Ricoh Americas, where he led strategic imaging research initiatives for seven years. 
Over 40 U.S. patents and publications evidence Kartheek's commitment to technological innovation in computational imaging and computer vision. He holds a Master's in Electrical Engineering and is an IEEE Senior Member.

Lyz Lewis
Head of Finance
Lyz joined Chef in 2025 to lead the Finance function with 7+ years experience specifically in robotics, automation, AI, and high-growth technology companies. About Us | Dexterity

Meet Dexterity
The company putting intelligence into the physical world.

Why We Exist
Digital AI generates text and images. We close the loop - from perception to reasoning to action in the real world.
Dexterity was founded on a conviction: that the next great leap in AI isn't another chatbot - it's giving machines the intelligence to operate in unstructured physical environments with the dexterity, judgment, and safety that the real world demands.

Our Journey

2017
Founded
Samir Menon founds Dexterity in Redwood City, California - assembling a team of Stanford roboticists with a singular bet: that AI could give robots genuine dexterity, not just motion.

2021
First Autonomous Pick
Dexterity's AI completes its first fully autonomous robotic pick - the moment Physical AI moved from research to reality. The system perceived, reasoned, and acted without human intervention.

2022
First Enterprise Deployment
Mech deploys at its first Fortune 500 customer facility for autonomous truck loading. Dexterity becomes one of the first companies to put Physical AI into continuous production.

2023
10 Million Actions
Dexterity surpasses 10 million autonomous in-production actions across customer sites - each one a real decision made by AI in an unstructured physical environment.

2024
International Expansion
Operations expand to Japan through a strategic partnership with Sagawa Express and Sumitomo Corporation - bringing Physical AI to one of the world's most demanding logistics markets.

2025
100 Million Actions
Dexterity reaches 100 million autonomous in-production actions.

2026
Driving Enterprise Transformation
FedEx highlights Dexterity at its Investor Day as a key technology partner for the future of logistics.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

 

 
(Redirected from Dexterity)

 
 
 

Coordination of small muscles, particularly of the hands and fingers, with the eyes

"Dexterity" redirects here. For other uses, see Dexterity (disambiguation).

This article needs additional citations for verification. Please help improve this article by adding citations to reliable sources. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed.
Find sources: "Fine motor skill" – news · newspapers · books · scholar · JSTOR (August 2022) (Learn how and when to remove this message)

This article needs to be updated. The reason given is: There are no citations beyond the late 2000s or so. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. (August 2022)

Fine motor skill or dexterity is the coordination of small muscles in movement with the eyes, hands and fingers. The complex levels of manual dexterity that humans exhibit can be related to the nervous system. Fine motor skills aid in the growth of intelligence and develop continuously throughout the stages of human development.

Types of motor skills
[edit]

Writing is a fine motor skill as it requires subtle motions of the hand and fingers.
Motor skills are movements and actions of the bone structures.[1] Typically, they are categorised into two groups: gross motor skills and fine motor skills. Gross motor skills are involved in movement and coordination of the arms, legs, and other large body parts. They involve actions such as running, crawling and swimming. Fine motor skills are involved in smaller movements that occur in the wrists, hands, fingers, feet and toes. Specifically, single joint movements are fine motor movements and require fine motor skills. They involve smaller actions such as picking up objects between the thumb and finger, writing carefully, and blinking.

Developmental stages
[edit]

Main article: Childhood development of fine motor skills

Through each developmental stage, motor skills gradually develop. They are first seen during infancy, toddler-hood, preschool and school age. "Basic" fine motor skills gradually develop and are typically mastered between the ages of 6–12 in children. Fine motor skills develop with age and practice. If deemed necessary, occupational therapy can help improve overall fine motor skills.[2]

Infancy
[edit]

Early fine motor skills are involuntary reflexes.[3] The most notable involuntary reflex is the Darwinian reflex, a primitive reflex displayed in various newborn primates species. These involuntary muscle movements are temporary and often disappear after the first two months. After eight weeks, an infant will begin to voluntarily use fingers to touch. However, infants have not learned to grab at this stage.

Infant displaying the palmar grasp reflex
Hand–eye coordination begins to develop at two to five months. Infants begin to reach for and grasp objects at this age. In 1952, Piaget found that even before infants are able to reach for and successfully grasp objects they see, they demonstrate competent hand-mouth coordination. A study was done by Philippe Rochat at Emory University in 1992 to test the relation between progress in the control of posture and the developmental transition from two-handed to one-handed engagement in reaching. It was found that the object reached for needed to be controlled. The precision of the reach is potentially maximized when placed centrally. 

It was also found that the posture needed to be controlled because infants that were not able to sit on their own used bimanual reaches in all postural positions except sitting upright, where they would reach one-handed. As a result, their grasping phases will not have been maximized because of the decrease in body control. On the other hand, if the infant does not have body control, it would be hard for them to get a hold of an object because their reach will be limited.

When "non-sitting" infants reached bimanually, while seated upright, they often ended up falling forward. Regardless of whether they can self-sit, infants can adjust their two handed engagement in relation to the arrangement of the objects being reached for. Analysis of hand-to-hand distance during reaching indicates that in the prone and supine posture, non-sitting infants moved their hands simultaneously towards the midline of their bodies as they reached which is not observed by stable sitting infants in any position. Non-sitter infants, although showing strong tendencies toward bimanual reaching, tend to reach with one hand when sat. Sitter infants show a majority of differentiated reaches in all posture conditions.

A study conducted by Esther Thelen on postural control during infancy used the dynamic systems approach to observe motor development. The findings suggest that early reaching is constrained by head and shoulder instability. The relationship between posture and reaching is tight. Thus, head control and body stability are necessary for the emergence of grasping.[citation needed]

The next developmental milestone is between seven and twelve months, when a series of fine motor skills begins to develop. These include increase in grip, enhancement of vision, pointing with the index finger, smoothly transferring objects from one hand to the other, as well as using the pincer grip (with the thumb and index fingers) to pick up tiny objects with precision. A lot of factors change in grasping when the infant becomes seven months. The infant will have a better chance of grasping because they can sit up. Therefore, the infant will not fall over. The infant grasping also changes. The infant starts to hold objects more properly when age increases.[4]

Toddlerhood
[edit]

Writing abilities are a major fine motor skill.
By the time a child is one year old, their fine motor skills have developed to hold and look at objects. As children manipulate objects with purpose, they gain experience identifying objects based on their shape, size, and weight. This develops the child's fine motor skills, and their understanding of the world.[5] A toddler will show hand dominance.

Preschool
[edit]

Children typically attend preschool between the ages of 2 and 5. At this time, the child is capable of grasping objects using the static tripod grasp, which is the combined use of the index, thumb, and middle finger. A preschool child's motor skills are moderate, allowing the child to cut shapes out of paper, draw or trace over vertical lines with crayons, button their clothes, and pick up objects. A preferred hand dominates the majority of their activities. They also develop sensory awareness and interpret their environment by using their senses and moving accordingly.[6]

After the static tripod grasp, the next form is the dynamic tripod grasp. These are shown in a series through Schneck and Henderson's Grip Form chart. Based on the accuracy and form of hold the child will be ranked either from 1–10 or 1–5 of how well they are able to complete the dynamic tripod grasp while properly writing. In conjunction with accuracy and precision the child will be able to properly position a writing utensil in terms of implement diameter as well as form and grip strength. Proper handwriting and drawing fall deeper into a category of graphomotor skills.[7]

The National Centre of Teaching and Learning illustrates the abilities that preschool children should have improved through their fine motor skills in several domains. Children use their motor skills by sorting and manipulating geometric shapes, making patterns, and using measurement tools to build their math skills. By using writing tools and reading books, they build their language and literacy. Arts and crafts activities like cutting and gluing paper, finger painting, and dressing up develops their creativity. Parents can support this development by intervening when the child does not perform the fine motor activity correctly, making use of several senses in a learning activity, and offer activities that the child will be successful with.[6]

Developmental disabilities such as autism or cerebral palsy may stop a child doing things that involve motor skills such as drawing or building blocks.[8] Fine motor skills acquired during this stage aids in the later advancement and understanding of subjects such as science and reading.[9] A study by the American Journal of Occupational Therapy, which included twenty-six preschool children who had received occupational therapy on a weekly basis, showed overall advancements in their fine motor skill area. The results showed a link between in-hand manipulation, hand–eye coordination, and grasping strength with the child's motor skills, self-care and social function. These children were shown to have better mobility and self-sustainment.[2]

School age
[edit]

During the ages between five and seven, the fine motor skills will have developed. As the child interacts with objects the movements of the elbows and shoulders should be less apparent, as should the movements of wrist and fingers. From the ages of three to five years old, girls advance their fine motor skills more than boys. Girls develop physically at an earlier age than boys; this is what allows them to advance their motor skills at a faster rate during prepubescent ages. Boys advance in gross motor skills later on at around age five and up. Children should be able to make precise cuts with scissors, for example, cutting out squares and holding them in a more common and mature manner. The child's movements should become fluid as the arms and hands become more in sync with each other. The child should also be able to write more precisely on lines, and print letters and numbers with greater clarity.

Common problems
[edit]

Fine motor skills can become impaired due to injury, illness, stroke, congenital deformities, cerebral palsy, or developmental disabilities. Problems with the brain, spinal cord, peripheral nerves, muscles, or joints can also have an effect on fine motor skills, and can decrease control. If an infant or child up to age five is not developing their fine motor skills, they will show signs of difficulty controlling their hands, fingers, and face. In young children, delays in learning sitting or walking is an early sign that there will be issues with fine motor skills, and may also show signs of difficulty with tasks such as cutting with scissors, drawing lines, or folding clothes. If a child has difficulty with these, they might have poor hand–eye coordination and could need therapy to improve their skills.[citation needed]

Assessment
[edit]

Fine motor skills can be assessed with standardized and non-standardized tests in children and adults. Fine-motor assessments can include force matching tasks. Humans exhibit a high degree of accuracy in force matching tasks where an individual is instructed to match a reference force applied to a finger with the same or different finger.[10] Humans show high accuracy during grip force matching tasks.[11] These aspects of manual dexterity are apparent in the ability of humans to effectively use tools, and perform hard manipulation tasks such as handling unstable objects.[12] Another assessment is called The Peabody Developmental Scales (PDMS).[13] PDMS is a test for children 0–7 that examines the child's ability to grasp a variety of objects, the development of hand–eye coordination, and the child's overall finger dexterity.[13]

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

 

 

 
 
 

American space technology company

LeoLabs is an American space technology company specializing in the tracking of satellites and space debris in low Earth orbit (LEO).[1] Founded in 2016 as a spin-off from SRI International, LeoLabs is headquartered in Menlo Park, California.[2]

The radar network enables tracking of objects as small as 2 centimeters, contributing significantly to collision avoidance and space traffic safety in the increasingly congested LEO environment.[3] As of late 2025, LeoLabs operates 11 radar installations across seven global sites, with locations in the United States (including Alaska, Texas, and Arizona), New Zealand, Costa Rica, Australia, Portugal’s Azores archipelago, and Argentina.[4][5][6][7]

In June 2021, the company completed a $65 million Series B financing round.[8] Financing has continued with an additional $29 million raised in February 2024.[9][10][11]

The company secured more than $50 million in contracts during 2024, marking nearly 140% revenue growth from the previous year.[12][13] In the first half of 2024 alone, LeoLabs won over $20 million in new contracts to deliver space domain awareness and traffic management services to U.S. and allied government agencies, as well as commercial satellite operators.[14]

About Us
Our Mission, Our Vision and Our Team

Skyryse is an aviation hardware and software company ushering in a new era in flight safety. Its proprietary technology, SkyOS™ is a universal operating system for flight – which powers its first aircraft, the Skyryse One™ – giving pilots greater control by simplifying the management of an aircraft during standard flight operations, inclement weather, and emergencies. 
Founded in 2016, Skyryse has accomplished several industry firsts made possible by SkyOS, including the world’s first automated takeoff, hover, landing, and automated engine-out landing of a helicopter, all controlled with the swipe of a finger.

Our Values
Safety
We value safety above all else, both for our employees advancing our mission and our customers who trust our technology with their lives.

Simplicity
We believe reducing unnecessary complexity increases clarity, minimizes opportunities for error, and streamlines our path to success. 

Quality
We are guided by the highest standards of excellence, as the consequences of failure in our mission are very real, in both lives and resources.

Integrity
We are commited to a culture of trust, accountability, and doing things the right way, all of which is essential for long term success.

Collaboration
We understand that collaboration on collective goals is mission critical as we solve the most difficult and complex problems.

Our Origin Story

As an engineer with a Ph.D. in sensor data fusion and 60+ patents for aerial vehicle automation, Founder and CEO Mark Groden has always been obsessed with flying machines. 
As a teenager, Mark began pilot training, where he came to understand the complexity of flying mechanics, and more critically, the challenges and often fatal consequences pilots face when things go wrong.
Having experienced the technology gap in aviation firsthand, Mark decided to found an aviation technology company focused on the industry’s number one problem – flight safety.

About | Dusty Robotics

Our industry

As the largest industry globally, construction builds the places we live, work, and play. Despite its significance, the industry faces pressing challenges including a labor shortage, escalating costs, and stringent timelines. With robots deployed throughout the US, Dusty is changing the landscape of the industry with high impact solutions that propel growth .

Our solution

Dusty enables the construction industry to build more with less, increasing construction efficiency while making job sites safer. Our flagship product, the FieldPrinter, automates layout with the most accurate mobile robot on the planet giving contractors the confidence that what they design is what they build.

Our values

Dusty Robotics was born from the belief that innovation and creativity are necessary to create revolutionary change. This mindset propels us to challenge the status quo and drive the construction industry into a new era of progress and excellence.

Professionalism

Dusty’s contractor-grade solutions are the tool of choice for professionals. On the job site, Dusty looks and acts professional. In the office, we treat each other with respect.

Trust

Our tools are used for mission-critical work on the most demanding jobs. Contractors trust Dusty to deliver. Within our team, we create a safe environment and trust each person to do their part to make us successful.

Simplicity

Our products are easy to use by anyone in the construction industry. We value simple engineering designs that are easy to understand, build, and maintain. We communicate with each other using plain, clear language.

Playfulness

Our products are fun to use and full of personality. We approach our work with a sense of lighthearted humor, without taking ourselves too seriously. We believe that creative, out-of-the-box thinking leads to better outcomes.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

 

 

 
 
 

Male given name

For the camcorder developed by Chinese technology company DJI, see Osmo (camera).

For the educational video game system, see Osmo (game system).

Osmo is a Finnish male given name. It appears in Kalevala, where it means "a young man". The name has been in use since the 1880s. The name day for Osmo in Finland is 11 May.[1]

[edit]

Osmo Kontula (born 1951), Finnish sociologist

Osmo Pekonen (1960–2022), Finnish mathematician

Osmo Tapio Räihälä (born 1964), Finnish composer

Osmo Soininvaara (born 1951), politician, previous member of the Finnish government

Osmo Vänskä (born 1953), Finnish conductor

Osmo Valtonen (1929–2002), Finnish artist

LabGenius Therapeutics | ML-driven Antibody Discovery

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 

 
 

Skip to content

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Combining human and machine intelligence to discover the next generation of therapeutic antibodies.
 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Therapeutic Challenge
 

 

 

 

 
Molecule performance is governed by a complex interplay of factors
 

 

 

 

 
Complex antibodies, like multispecifics, are hard to engineer because the relationship between design and function is often non-intuitive. As a consequence, conventional methods are liable to deliver sub-optimal molecules which either fail in the clinic or result in poor patient outcomes.
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Our Solution
 

 

 

 

 
Introducing the EVA™ platform
 

 

 

 

 
We’ve developed EVA™ — a highly automated discovery engine that enables the rapid identification and co-optimisation of complex therapeutic antibodies across multiple key properties. 
 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 
 
 Learn More
 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Therapeutic Impact
 

 

 

 

 
Complete on/off killing selectivity for therapeutic antibodies 

 

 

 

 

 
Our platform is both modality and format-agnostic but for our internal pipeline, we’re focussed on the development of selectivity-enhanced antibodies for the treatment of solid tumours. Our novel approach is delivering a pipeline of antibody therapeutics with best-in-class killing profiles.

Home - Cresilon
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 

 

 
 
 


 
 
 




 Skip to content

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 
A world where bleeding is no longer a risk
 

 

 

 

 
Our mission is to save lives. We provide a safe, effective, and reliable solution to stop bleeding.
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
0

 
lives are lost due to uncontrolled bleeding each year*

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 
1.75M

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
*Source: WHO 2024
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Our Mission is to Save Lives
 

 

 

 

 
Uncontrolled bleeding continues to pose a significant global health challenge, highlighting the need for more effective solutions for rapid and dependable hemorrhage control
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Trauma

 

 

 Hemorrhage is responsible for over 80% of operating-room deaths after major trauma.
 

 

 It also accounts for the largest proportion of mortality in the first hour of trauma center care and nearly
 50% of deaths in the first 24 hours.
 

 

 Source: Kauvar, David S. MD; Lefering, Rolf PhD; Wade, Charles E. PhD. Impact of Hemorrhage on Trauma Outcome: An Overview of Epidemiology, Clinical Presentations, and Therapeutic Considerations. The Journal of Trauma: Injury, Infection, and Critical Care 60(6):p S3-S11, June 2006. | DOI: 10.1097/01.ta.0000199961.02677.19
 

 

 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
Defense · National Security

 

 

 91% of potentially survivable battlefield deaths
 are related to hemorrhage.
 

 

 Rapid hemorrhage control is therefore a primary determinant of survivability in combat settings.
 

 

 Source: Eastridge B.J. et al., The Journal of Trauma and Acute Care Surgery, 2012
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 
 

 

 
THE CRITICAL TECHNOLOGY GAP

 

 Cresilon closes the critical technology gap by delivering rapid, reliable hemostasis when every second matters.
 The hydrogel technology works fast with no-preparation and controls bleeding in seconds by adapting seamlessly to a wide range of wound types and geometries, ensuring consistent performance across diverse clinical scenarios.
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Our Solution
 

 

 

 

 
Cresilon has developed the world’s first flowable hemostatic gel technology that achieves hemostasis in seconds for even the most severe hemorrhage
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 
TRAUMAGEL®

 
TRAUMAGEL® is a hemostatic gel designed to control external moderate to severe bleeding in seconds for EMS Agencies, Trauma Hospitals, Military, and Law Enforcement.

 Learn more
 

 

 
VETIGEL®

 
VETIGEL® is the first hemostatic gel in the veterinary market that stops bleeding in seconds. VETIGEL® has been used in 65,000 procedures across the globe.

 Learn more
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Testimonials

 

 
 
 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
About Cresilon
 

 

 

 

 
Cresilon develops advanced hemostatic gel technology that controls bleeding in seconds. Founded and headquartered in Brooklyn, NY.
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Our Products
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Our plant-based technology has revolutionized the current standard of care by stopping traumatic and surgical bleeds in seconds without the need for manual pressure. Cresilon’s current and future product lines target veterinary, human trauma, and human surgical applications. 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 Emergency Medicine
 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 Animal Health
 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Our Story
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Constantly experimenting with natural materials in the lab as a young adult, Joe Landolina developed an adhesive hemostatic gel composed of plant-based polymers that could support the natural clotting process.
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 Learn more
 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Technology in Action
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 
 
 

 
 
 
 Your browser does not support the video tag.
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Latest News

 

 

 

 
 
 
 
 
 

 
Cresilon Announces TRAUMAGEL Presentations at the 2026 Special Operations Medical Association Scientific Assembly

 
Cresilon announces two research presentations that will be featured at the 2026 Special Operations Medical Association (SOMA) Scientific Assembly April 27–May 1 in Raleigh, North Carolina, highlighting the real-world use of TRAUMAGEL® in prehospital trauma care settings.

 
 Read more

The Dominance of Digital
The future is open. At Parry Labs, we believe capability for combat advantage relies on the speed and scale of open systems. As the industry leaders in Modular Open Systems Approach, we are redefining the edge for the modern battlespace through fast, secure and future-proof digital systems integration.


 

 

 
 

Meet The Most Agile Software Stack For Your Mission
Parry Labs introduces STRATIA® – the industry leading software operating environment for systems integration.

Featuring secure, best-in-class container management, application hosting, intelligent networking and tools for best of breed components. A critical part of our holistic, modular capability platform for both safety-critical and mission-critical systems.

Read More

Small Form Factor Compute Power To The Edge
Parry’s ultra-compact hardware solutions leverage a rich heritage of relay functionality with the highest level of environmental specifications. They provide unparalleled compute power and high-density input/output for application hosting of autonomy, intelligent networking, artificial intelligence/machine learning, navigation capabilities and more.

See our Products

The Future of Mission Readiness
The Virtual Integration Workspace (VIW) is a powerful platform for collaborative manipulation of digital twins to enable cloud-based mission system emulation for rapid integration and evaluation of capabilities.

Learn More

Global Expeditionary Miniature Mission Interface (GEMMI)
GEMMI is a secure multi-platform Control System, Communications Hub, and Mission Support Solution to reduce logistics to a single platform while increasing capability in contested environments at the edge. Parry is a lead developer for modernized ground solutions to simultaneously control multiple, disparate types of unmanned or optionally manned aircraft and payloads.

Datasheet


 

 

 
 

Mission Systems
Inserting Parry Labs hardware and software solutions into your platform allows best-of-breed capability between platform provider, mission application vendors, and multi-domain networking to drastically reduce deployment timelines to accelerate new platforms and modernize legacy systems for the future.  

Learn More

Modular Open Systems Approach
STRATIA® provides a framework that considers the Modular Open Systems Approach (MOSA) of each service and domain up front so common capabilities can be re-used across platforms to provide safety and mission critical operating environments.

Modular Open Systems Approach

Our Leadership

 

 

 

 
 

 
Chief Executive Officer, Co-Founder & Board Member


 

 

 
John “JD” Parkes
Chief Executive Officer, Co-Founder & Board Member
Parkes founded Parry Labs to design, deploy and operate creative solutions for national security. As an entrepreneur, he serves as Co-Founder, Chief Strategy Officer and board member of Skydweller Aero, Inc. and has extensive experience in operational leadership and business development in government and commercial markets. Prior to starting Parry Labs, he has held the position of Airborne Mission Lead at the Office of Strategic Capabilities within the U.S. Department of Defense, worked in Strategic Planning, Corporate Development and M&A at United Technologies and was an image scientist on classified programs at Harris.


 


 

 

 
 

 
Chief Financial Officer


 

 

 
Jon Pilon
Chief Financial Officer
Jon Pilon joined Parry Labs in 2022, bringing financial leadership, strategic business partner and audit experience in privately held and publicly traded companies with a mix of debt and equity capital structures. He has been a key leader in high-growth government contracting, distribution, technology systems and services companies. Prior to joining Parry Labs, Jon served as the Chief Financial Officer of Federal Resources Supply Company, a private-equity backed government contractor, from 2015 through a strategic sale in late 2021. 
 
Jon currently serves on the board of directors of a Michigan-based government contractor serving the Department of Defense. Jon is a CPA and earned a Bachelor of Arts in Mathematics from the College of the Holy Cross.


 


 

 

 
 

 
Chief Growth Officer


 

 

 
Amber Walker
Chief Growth Officer
As Chief Growth Officer, Amber leads Parry Labs’ growth strategy across all channels, focusing on market expansion, customer engagement, and long-term value creation. Previously, she served as Parry Labs’ Senior Vice President of Ground, Maritime, C2, and Maritime Systems. 

Dr. Walker brings a wealth of experience in the Defense sector, from both government and industry roles, to working on advanced technology development and winning new business. She most recently served as the Technical Director of Anduril Industries’ Land Systems business and played a pivotal role at Raytheon as Deputy Business Unit Lead in the Network & Cyber Technologies sector. 

During her career in the U.S. Army, Amber was a DARPA Program Manager at the Tactical Technology Office. She currently a Lieutenant Colonel in the U.S Army Reserves within the Defense Innovation Unit and is on the Army Science Board and an active member of the American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics. 


 


 

 

 
 

 
Senior Vice President, Growth Operations & General Manager, Huntsville


 

 

 
Tom von Eschenbach
Senior Vice President, Growth Operations & General Manager, Huntsville
Tom leads Parry’s crewed aviation programs across the DoD from the Huntsville site, overseeing program management, business development and engineering. He previously served as the Director for Program Management and Site Lead for Collins Aerospace’s Army Rotary Wing Avionics Solutions team where he managed Future Vertical Lift and MOSA pursuits. Tom served for more than 28 years in the Army commanding Soldiers at many levels deploying to both Operations Iraqi and Enduring Freedom. He currently serves on the Board of Directors for the Vertical Lift Consortium and as a member of the Auburn University Aerospace Engineering Advisory Council.

Autonomous Defense,
Unmatched Protection

 

 

 
Fortem delivers advanced radar systems and autonomous drones that safeguard the airspace and ensure the safety of military and civilian operations from aerial threats.

 
 
Learn More

 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 
 
 

 
 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Redefining C-UAS. Leading and providing for AAM.

 

Fortem Technologies develops solutions and strategies in C-UAS (counter-unmanned aerial systems), ISR (intelligence, reconnaissance, surveillance), perimeter security, airspace safety, and several emerging airborne markets.

Best known for our TrueView® radars and autonomous DroneHunter® interceptors, we work with military, government, and commercial customers to solve drone-related security and defense challenges while simultaneously laying the groundwork for AAM (advanced air mobility).

With backing from Toshiba, Boeing, DCVC, Signia Venture Partners, and other strategic partnerships, Fortem Technologies continues to push the limits of critical thinking and technical innovation, earning a reputation as a leader in airspace security and defense.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 

 
The SkyDome System®

 

The SkyDome System® is a proven counter-unmanned aircraft system (C-UAS) solution that monitors and protects airspace day or night and in adverse weather conditions. While many C-UAS deployments fail because of components from multiple vendors lacking compatibility and integration, Fortem has pioneered the technology required to build a fully integrated, end-to-end counter-drone solution.

Flexible and portable, the System can be deployed as a permanent installation or as part of a mobile strategy. It is already in use at world-class venues, military bases, airports, and infrastructures around the world.

SkyDome System® Components

 

Three deeply integrated product families comprise the SkyDome System®: TrueView® sensors, DroneHunter® interceptors, and SkyDome® Manager software. Each was engineered from the ground up to solve the problem of dangerous drones.

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 
 

 
 

 
 

 

 
TrueView® R40 Radar

 
 
High-performance, true Active Electronically Scanned Array (AESA) radar for dependable drone defense.

 
 

 

 

 
 

 

 
 

 
 

 
 

 

 
TrueView® R30 Radar

 
 
High-performance, ground-based AESA radar with the power of AI at the Edge. Designed for drone detection and classification.

 
 

 

 

 
 

 

 
 

 
 

 
 

 

 
TrueView® R20 Radar

 
 
Compact air / ground AESA radar delivering on the promise of low SWaP. Designed for drone detection and classification.

 
 

 

 

 
 

 

 
 

 
 

 
 

 

 
TrueView® C30 Camera System

 
 
Long-range PTZ camera system, integrated to follow prioritized radar tracks and provide visual validation and optical classification.

 
 

 

 

 
 

 

 
 

 
 

 
 

 

 
DroneHunter® F700

 
 
The safe, intelligent way to defeat dangerous drones. Performs mid-air captures autonomously with an impressive 85% success rate.

 
 

 

 

 
 

 

 
 

 
 

 
 

 

 
DroneHangar™

 
 
Automated launch deck and secure, weatherproof storage for DroneHunter® interceptors. Units are dispatched in seconds, ready for a fight.

 
 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 
View All Products

 

 

 

 
 

 
 

 

 

 
Solutions for Safer Skies

 

Whether it’s flown by a careless operator or a ferocious terrorist, an unauthorized drone entering restricted airspace poses a serious problem for any organization. Fortem Technologies has the equipment, foresight, and experience to implement highly effective counter-drone solutions thoughtfully tailored to the area needing protection, no matter its size or nature.

 

 

 

 
 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Advanced Airspace Awareness 

 

 
Learn More

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Airports

 
With passenger jets coming and going every minute, airports are a prime target for malicious, drone-based attacks. Our solution enables air traffic controllers to keep small airborne intruders far away from terminals and runways.

 
Learn More

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Energy

 
The energy and industrial sectors have definite cause for concern over trespassing drones, which can be a hazard to workers, at best, or the reason for a terrible accident, at worst. Our solution prevents either of these outcomes.

 
Learn More

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Law Enforcement

 
As more criminals begin using store-bought drones to commit serious felonies, it is imperative that law enforcement agencies have the tools to stop them. Our solution prepares today's police officers to deal with tomorrow's threats.

 
Learn More

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Defense

 
The role drones play on the battlefield continues to evolve, mainly because the good guys are no longer the only side with access to them. Our solution equips brave soldiers with technology that can detect and neutralize enemy UAVs.

 
Learn More

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Prisons

 
The existence of drones is an opportunity for incarcerated criminals and accomplices on the outside who intend to help them. Our solution prevents drones from delivering drugs, weapons, and other contraband to inmates.

 
Learn More

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Public Venues

 
Any place of gathering runs the risk of becoming a target for hateful individuals. Drones make evil's job easier, which is why every public venue should have a strong defense against them. Our solution protects innocent people from harm.

 
Learn More

Fortem Technologies has the equipment, foresight, and experience to implement highly effective counter-drone solutions thoughtfully tailored to the area needing protection, no matter its size or nature.

 

 

 

 
 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Advanced Airspace Awareness 

 

 
Learn More

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Airports

 
With passenger jets coming and going every minute, airports are a prime target for malicious, drone-based attacks. Our solution enables air traffic controllers to keep small airborne intruders far away from terminals and runways.

 
Learn More

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Energy

 
The energy and industrial sectors have definite cause for concern over trespassing drones, which can be a hazard to workers, at best, or the reason for a terrible accident, at worst. Our solution prevents either of these outcomes.

 
Learn More

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Law Enforcement

 
As more criminals begin using store-bought drones to commit serious felonies, it is imperative that law enforcement agencies have the tools to stop them. Our solution prepares today's police officers to deal with tomorrow's threats.

 
Learn More

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Defense

 
The role drones play on the battlefield continues to evolve, mainly because the good guys are no longer the only side with access to them. Our solution equips brave soldiers with technology that can detect and neutralize enemy UAVs.

 
Learn More

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Prisons

 
The existence of drones is an opportunity for incarcerated criminals and accomplices on the outside who intend to help them. Our solution prevents drones from delivering drugs, weapons, and other contraband to inmates.

 
Learn More

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Public Venues

 
Any place of gathering runs the risk of becoming a target for hateful individuals. Drones make evil's job easier, which is why every public venue should have a strong defense against them. Our solution protects innocent people from harm.

 
Learn More

Keeping us safe by sensing more.

Emergent technologies have centered the RF spectrum — the dimension where information and energy travels — as a high-stakes site for military engagement, while posing risks and opportunities for the civilian world. 

We understand the strategic importance of mastering this hidden domain. That’s why we’re building the most advanced RF sensing technology on the planet: to bring clarity and confidence to the critical decisions you face every day.

We don’t just build technology for technology’s sake. We build for a better purpose. For a future where our people and places are safeguarded. Where our cities are safer and more connected. And where our freedoms are protected.

 
 

 
 

The values that drive our work.

Always for your advantage.

Our technical advances power your competitive advantage.

What matters most is getting you the right data at the right time so you can take clear, decisive action. That’s why we engineer bleeding-edge technology – so you can retain your competitive edge.

Functional tech over fictional tech.

We pride ourselves in delivering relevant tech that’s smart, easy, and reliable.

We care about quality first, engineering all our products with a relentless eye for detail and durability. This persistent methodology is what keeps us focused, and consistently industry-leading.

Transparency & security you can trusT.

We take our partnerships personally.

That’s why we’re committed to transparency in the way we talk, the way we build, and the way we collaborate. And that’s why we work so hard to secure your privacy, deliver projects on time and on budget, and assure you have the tools you need to succeed.

Here to do good.

We don’t just build technology for technology’s sake. We build for a better purpose.

For a future where our cities are safer and more connected. Where our people and places are safeguarded. And where our freedoms are protected.

Always reaching for the next level.

About MacroFab
 
A Better Way to Build Electronics
 
A redefined experience, innovative technology, scale, and agility. Rethinking contract manufacturing from the ground up.
 
 
 
 
 
 
Our History
 
Built by Engineers Who Felt the Pain
 
MacroFab was founded by engineers who experienced the frustrations of outdated electronics manufacturing firsthand. Opaque pricing, slow turnaround, and disconnected tooling were the norm. They set out to change it.
 
The answer was software. By rebuilding the quoting, sourcing, and production workflow on a modern technology platform, MacroFab made it possible to go from design files to a production-ready order in minutes, not days.
 
 
 
Our Future
 
Transforming a $440B Industry
 
MacroFab has built a technology platform connecting thousands of businesses to a network of manufacturing facilities. The contract electronics manufacturing industry is large, fragmented, and overdue for modernization.
 
Headquartered in Houston, Texas, home to a diverse industrial ecosystem spanning energy, aerospace, manufacturing, and medicine, MacroFab is positioned to give engineering and procurement teams greater production control, faster time to market, and lower total costs.

Welcoming Hiten Sonpal: RISE® Robotics' New CEO
RISE is thrilled to introduce Hiten Sonpal as its new CEO. With an impressive background in robotics and automation, Hiten brings a wealth of experience from his roles at Electric Sheep Robotics, Mowbot, and iRobot.
In his first month, Hiten has already begun implementing a strategic vision focused on innovation and rapid market impact. One notable initiative is the introduction of an "innovation time" carve-out, allowing employees to dedicate Friday afternoons to passion projects that could drive technological advancements.
Hiten’s vision for RISE is clear: to revolutionize industrial machinery by replacing traditional hydraulic systems with our cutting-edge belt technology. This shift promises greater productivity and efficiency, aligning with global sustainability goals.
With a dynamic team and Hiten’s leadership, RISE is poised for significant growth and innovation. Stay tuned as we embark on this exciting new chapter.

 

 

 
 
With years of experience in a wide range of senior leadership roles in high technology fields, including robotics, space propulsion, semiconductor testing, and telecommunications, Tom Phleps, now RISE® COO, was looking for a new opportunity that would bring him back to his roots in robotics and heavy hardware.
He was searching for a revolutionary technology with the potential to change an industry and a committed and brilliant founding team.
"RISE Robotics checked all of those boxes and aligned well with the next step in my career," he points out.
"It's a fascinating place to be," he adds. "We are at a point where there are more opportunities than bandwidth. Part of my role is filtering those opportunities and ensuring we can meet and exceed our customers' expectations. I want to ensure we over-deliver to customers and build the RISE Robotics brand and reputation.
"RISE® Technology is truly revolutionary and will enable the development of the highest-performance and most efficient heavy machinery on the market. I look forward to helping RISE grow and become an industry technology leader."

To hear more about Tom's journey to RISE and what has him so excited for the company's future, listen to "InsideRISE® - Tom Phelps" live on the RISE Inside podcast!

 

 

 
 
Co-Founder, VP of Business Development, and former CEO of RISE® Robotics, Arron Acosta, joins host Justin Starbird to discuss how RISE® Robotics is the future of machinery.
As industrial machine collaborators, we need much more from hydraulic systems. We need something just as powerful, but far more efficient, reliable, and durable.
We challenged the status quo and developed RISE® Cylinder - a ground-breaking alternative that simply works.
RISE® Cylinder is a fluid-free, electro-mechanical alternative to hydraulic cylinders for heavy equipment and machinery that addresses the battery-electrification problems inherent in hydraulic systems by delivering hydraulic-like forces at unprecedented speed, weight, precision and efficiency.
RISE® Cylinder uses up to 90% less energy compared to hydraulic cylinders and recharges batteries through regeneration.

RISE® Cylinder compacts steel reinforced polyurethane belts that revolutionized high-speed elevators into a package that replaces a hydraulic cylinder.

RISE® Cylinder provides continuous remote monitoring of system health, predictive maintenance and performance.

Listen as Arron tells Justin about the early history of RISE® , and discusses some of the incredible steps that have led to where we are today.

 

 

 
 
Blake Sessions, co-Founder & Chief Technology Officer, and Ken Gray, former Vice President of Business Development at RISE® Robotics join host, Justin Starbird to share more about the early days of RISE®, its foundation, and where it's headed.
Blake explains that as a group, "RISE® just loves efficient, effective machine design. We're here to reimagine how machines are designed from an architectural perspective and from a usability perspective. The energy at RISE® centers around making machines that are just beautiful to use."
Listen as Blake and Ken tell Justin about RISE®'s unique intersection of elegant mathematics and practical applications to build beautiful machines.

 

 

 
 
MIT-educated engineer, co-founder of RISE® Robotics, and current VP of Engineering Toomas Sepp has been a part of the company's evolution from its initial exoskeleton concept to the recent launch of its first commercial product in partnership with Anthony Liftgates, the ReGEN RAILTRAC with RISE® Inside.
"It's been an exciting journey over the years, constantly trying to find what RISE’s place is in the marketplace. We have a pretty coherent and compelling story on the technology front, but it's just as exciting now to see how we fit that technology into the value chain.
"Over the next 5 to 10 years, we plan to make several different load classes of electric linear actuators to provide OEMs and integrators with that base, that component, that they can use to make higher-performing, more efficient, lower-total-cost-of-ownership machines.
"It's exciting to know that now we can invest in personnel, person hours, engineering hours, and partner relationships, which will ultimately yield even bigger results and more growth at the end of the day. That growth will lead to an impact in the real world and measurable results in lower energy usage in the machines that our technology ends up taking a foothold in."

To hear more about the evolution of RISE™Technology and the company's vision for the future, listen to "InsideRISE™ - Toomas Sepp" live on the RISE Inside podcast.

 

 

 
 
Co-Founder & Head of Industrial Design and Customer Experience Kyle Dell'Aquila and former Vice President of Business Development Ken Gray join host Justin Starbird for a deep dive into the unique story that is the history of RISE®. They talk about the RISE® philosophy and approach to investment and how it differs from most companies in similar situations.
Kyle and Ken explain that the bulk of hydraulics and the limitations of linear actuators drove the brilliant team at RISE® to develop something electric that had much more capacity and capability than electric actuators. To deliver the performance of hydraulics, that is, the force, the speed, the stroke without the bulk.
This creative perspective of the ever-evolving RISE® story inspires even the non-engineering folks to defy the rules to create new solutions as we explore and understand the incredible technology that is RISE®.

 

 

 
 
As a student at Northeastern University with a couple of co-ops under his belt, Nolan Hobart was looking for the next step on his journey—one that would allow him to build a solid technical base as well as an opportunity to work in the green space.
RISE® Robotics provided him with that.
"Boarding the rocket ship, 30 seconds before blast off," as he describes it, Nolan joined the founders in testing and designing their alpha cylinder, a demo unit for a relationship they were chasing with Anthony Liftgates.

Three years later, as the company prepares to launch its first commercial product in partnership with Anthony Liftgates, the ReGEN RAILTRAC with RISE™Inside, a revolutionary all-electric liftgate, Nolan finds himself in the position of Mechanical Engineer and assumes much of the technical responsibilities as an essential member of the Liftgate team.
"Throughout this journey, I grew confident to make a decision, know that it was the right one, or at least the best one at the time, and go from there. I've recently been operating with much more ownership over my design decisions. Now, I'm confident and have the technical prowess to back it up."

To hear more about Nolan's vision of RISE Robotics and how his love for snowmobiling at a young age ignited his engineering career, listen to "InsideRISE- Nolan Hobart" live on the RISE Inside podcast now!

 

 

 
 
With a combined Mechanical Engineering and Experience Design major from Northeastern University, Erika Murray, Mechanical Engineer & Engineering Project Manager, joined the RISE® team with a vision centered on people and their experiences to drive design, processes, environments, and strategies.
"Half of the time, I'm in the CAD world doing mechanical testing and designing systems to prepare us for manufacturing. The other half of the time, I'm helping the team focus on how they can improve, what tools we can gather, and what methods we can put in place to make us the best we can be."
Following her passion for sustainability to RISE also makes her days very fulfilling.
"The thing that hooked me was that this technology is in everything. We're designing cylinders that are everywhere you look. Everything could be based on them. The idea that, through our technology, everything can be a little bit greener was exciting to me.
"At the end of the day, all I need to say is that it was good work. I know that the work I put in is valuable. If it's a long day, then all the better that it was worthwhile work that I spent that time on.
"I'm proud to say that I work at RISE."

To hear more about Erika's journey, her vision for success, and her work at RISE, listen to "InsideRISE™- Erika Murray" live on the RISE Inside podcast now!

FAQ’s

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 
We are always here to answer your questions

 

 

 

 
 
 Where are parts produced?
 
 
 

 

 
All parts are produced domestically in Austin, Texas and shipped tariff-free within the U.S.

 

 

 
 
 
 What makes this different from traditional foundries?
 
 
 

 

 
We eliminate tooling and minimum order constraints by using Digital Metal Casting to 3D print ceramic molds, enabling unparalleled geometric freedom. This also allows faster lead times, lower upfront costs, and greater flexibility.

 

 

 
 
 
 Is there a minimum order quantity?
 
 
 

 

 
No. We support single prototypes through to production volumes — without traditional tooling commitments.

 

 

 
 
 
 How quickly can I receive parts?
 
 
 

 

 
Lead times depend on geometry and quantity, but our process significantly reduces the time typically associated with tooling and overseas sourcing. We can deliver castings within a week.

 

 

 
 
 
 What alloys do you offer?
 
 
 

 

 
We offer an ever expanding range of alloys suitable for production applications. Contact us for a full list and recommendations based on your use case.
Aluminum
Zinc
Brass
Stainless Steel
Copper
Nickel

 

 

 
 
 
 What tolerances can you achieve?
 
 
 

 

 
Tolerances depend on part geometry and alloy. We review each CAD file and provide guidance during quoting to ensure manufacturability. We typically hold +/- 0.1mm/100mm.

 

 

 
 
 
 How does Digital Metal Casting work?
 
 
 

 

 
We 3D print a precision ceramic mold directly from your CAD file, then cast the part in your selected alloy. This removes the need for permanent tooling while maintaining production-grade quality.

 

 

 
 
 
 Can I bring Digital Metal Casting in house?
 
 
 

 

 
Absolutely, get in touch to join the waitlist for a DMC System™.

Resilience Starts at the Atomic Level.

 

 
We believed atomic-level precision was the key to advancing the world’s most critical infrastructure. So, we built a platform that delivers it at scale.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
TRUSTED BY

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 
 
 

 

 
 
 
 
 

 

 
 
 
 
 

 

 
 
 
 
 

 

 
 
 
 
 

 

 
 
 
 
 

 

 
 
 
 
 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 
MISSION

Strengthening Critical Infrastructure, One Atom at a Time

 

 

 
The world’s most critical systems demand new levels of performance, safety, and reliability.

Our Atomic Armor™ platform, enabled by our patented ALDˣ architecture, is redefining what’s possible at the atomic level. A scalable, adaptable technology that strengthens the systems powering modern life, from energy and defense to advanced technologies.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
The Atomic Armor™ Platform

 

 

Thermal instability and surface degradation represent the final bottlenecks for modern hardware, causing failure long before design reaches its limit. Atomic Armor™ is the industrial-scale platform that removes these constraints at the atomic level. We deliver faster-charging batteries, flawless semiconductor yields, and high-strength structural components—reinforcing the material foundations of national security to ensure the world’s most critical systems perform at scale.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 
AI Hardware

AI performance is

limited by materials,

not models.

 

 

 
AI accelerators don’t fail because of algorithms — they fail because of physics. Thermal instability, leakage, and interface defects cap performance long before models do.

If you improve those, you enable:

higher performance per watt

more reliable high-density logic

reduced thermal degradation

lower defect rates

longer hardware lifetimes

That’s why Atomic Armor™ doesn’t just support AI hardware — it reinforces the material foundations enabling faster, more efficient compute at scale.

That’s why Atomic Armor™ doesn’t just support quantum hardware — it removes the material bottlenecks that prevents quantum systems from scaling.

 

 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 
 

 

 

 
Proof In Action

 

 

 

 

 

 
Forge Nano to Produce 100% US-Sourced Lithium-Ion Cells for US Department of Defense

 
 
Our Impact

 
Forge Nano is developing a lithium-ion battery cell for the US Department of Defense for testing that includes battery materials solely sourced from the United States. The 18650 cylindrical cell has high capacity and energy density, while meeting Department of Defense requirements for cycle life and operating temperatures.

 

 Learn More
 
 
 
 
 
 

 

 

 
Forge Nano Expands Semiconductor Business; Unveils new 200mm Wafer ALD Cluster Tool

 
 
Our Impact

 
TEPHRA is dedicated to the manufacturing of specialty semiconductor applications on 200mm wafers and below. With 100x efficient chemical use, rapid cycle times, increased yield, and low-risk manufacturing, TEPHRA is the only single-wafer cluster tool with commercial throughput speeds.

 

 Learn More
 
 
 
 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
America’s Gigafactory 

 

 

 

 
Defense

Aerospace

Grid Storage

Mobility Markets

 

 
Forge Nano’s North Carolina gigafactory is a transformational blueprint for U.S. energy security – delivering 100% domestic lithium-ion cell manufacturing at scale to meet surging demand from defense, aerospace, grid storage, and advanced mobility markets. Backed by a $100M U.S. Department of Energy grant and designed with proprietary Atomic Armor™ technology, the facility enables category-leading energy density, safety, and performance with unmatched manufacturing efficiency.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Solutions at Every Scale

 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Energy Storage

 
 

 
 

 

 
Lithium-Ion Batteries

 
 

 
Forge Nano lithium-ion cells deliver unmatched energy density, durability and cycle life at scale to enable more power in small, lighter formats – extending range, mission duration and overall system reliability. Forge Nano cells are produced in the U.S. with a U.S.-dominant material supply chain to establish a secure and scalable foundation for the future of U.S. energy independence.

 

 Batteries
 
 
 
 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Semiconductors

 
 

 
 

 

 
Commercial Wafer Fab Equipment

 
 

 
Forge Nano’s semiconductor wafer fabrication equipment applies ultra-thin, atomically precise coatings that unlock higher device performance, improved yield, and advanced material integration. Engineered for high-throughput production, these systems deliver conformal coatings at lower precursor consumption, reducing cost of ownership while enabling next-generation device architectures.

 

 Semiconductors
 
 
 
 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 

 

 

 

 
Material Coating

 
 

 
 

 

 
Powder ALD Equipment

 
 

 
Forge Nano’s powder ALD equipment enables precise, uniform coating of powders and granular materials at the atomic scale, transforming the performance of materials used in batteries, catalysts, pharmaceuticals, and advanced composites. By engineering surface properties without altering core material structure, it enhances conductivity, stability, and reactivity in ways traditional coating methods cannot achieve.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

 

 

 
 
 

Look up freeform in Wiktionary, the free dictionary.

Freeform or free-form may refer to:

Electron-beam freeform fabrication, an additive manufacturing process that builds near-net-shape parts

Free-form radio, a radio station programming format in which the disc jockey is given either total or wide control over what music to play

Freeform (Apple), a digital whiteboarding application developed by Apple

Freeform (TV channel), an American basic cable channel owned and operated by ABC Family Worldwide

Freeform crochet and knitting, a seemingly random combination of crochet, knitting and in some cases other fibre arts

Freeform Five, an English electronic group

Freeform role-playing game, a type of role-playing game which employ informal or simplified rule sets

Freeform surface modelling, a technique for engineering freeform surfaces with a CAD or CAID system

KFFP-LP (also Freeform Portland), a low-power listener supported community radio station in Portland, Oregon

A NEW ERA: PROGRAMMABLE OPTICS
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Light Control Metasurface™
Optical Beamforming Technology
 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 
The Era of Programmable Optics Has Begun
 

 

 

 

 
Beamforming is as easy as software
 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
No mirrors, no lenses? 
No problem.
 

 

 

 

 
The groundbreaking Light Control Metasurface (LCM™) technology empowers precise manipulation of light at a sub-micron level by dynamically shaping and steering light on the surface of a chip without relying on any moving parts. From 3D sensing to high-speed communications and switching, the LCM beamforming chip is revolutionizing the global approach to Programmable Optics. 
Best of all, it’s available today.
 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 
LCM Optical Beamforming
 

 

 

 

 
One Chip, Boundless Applications
 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 
Programmable Optics: Pioneering New Frontiers.
 

 

 

 

 
LCM optical beamforming excels in fields requiring spatial light manipulation. Its applications include optical communications in both terrestrial and space environments, as well as optical network switching in AI hyperscaler data centers. Lumotive is at the forefront of advancing optical technologies.
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Establish a new benchmark for industrial automation.
 

 

 

 

 
Lumotive’s LCM-enabled 3D sensing excels in diverse environments and complex tasks, ranging from advanced object tracking on factory floors to the dynamic navigational challenges encountered by modern service robots, all with remarkable flexibility and dependability.
 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 
Transform spaces into smart environments.
 

 

 

 

 
Lumotive’s software-defined scanning capability decisively transforms smart spaces by dynamically controlling range and resolution. This innovative technology enables effective real-time traffic monitoring, comprehensive urban flow analysis, and advanced environmental design.
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Enhance the standards of safety and autonomy.
 

 

 

 

 
Starting with today’s Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and moving towards a future with fully autonomous self-driving cars, Lumotive’s solid-state beamforming technology offers impressive durability, an extensive field-of-view, and flexibility to meet both short and long-range performance needs.
 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 
Scale consumer products without compromise.
 

 

 

 

 
LCM technology is revolutionizing the consumer electronics industry by providing the functionality of a scanning lidar sensor in ultra-compact formats. With cost-effective, silicon-based mass production and limitless scalability, it facilitates advanced 3D sensing in next-gen devices, leading to remarkable consumer experiences.
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 
Securing our future.
 

 

 

 

 
LCM Technology is vital for achieving SWaP-C in systems ranging from ultra-small to large-aperture Lidars and Agile Communications for both terrestrial and space applications. Optimizing size, weight, power, and cost is a critical aspect of defense systems.

Innovating the Future: Our Story
 

 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 Our Mission 

 

 

 

 
Lumotive is pioneering the era of programmable optics, where for the first time, light is controlled as intelligently and flexibly as software.
 

 

 

 

 Our patented Light Control Metasurface (LCM™) transforms photonics into a digital platform, unlocking breakthroughs in sensing, optical switching, and communication. With more than 160 patents and growing commercial traction, Lumotive is delivering the world’s first digital platform for light — and redefining what’s possible in the optical age.
